skill.name: clean-code-reviewer
skill.description: Analyze code quality with Clean Code principles. The skill reviews naming, function size, duplication, over-engineering, magic numbers, clarity, and project conventions, then returns severity-rated refactoring advice.
skill.summary: Review code for Clean Code issues and prioritized refactoring advice.
skill.author: hylarucoder

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and entropy alerts. Manual review found only Markdown documentation, JSON report text, and educational code examples; no executable skill code, prompt injection, network behavior, or system access was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to Markdown code fences, inline code, and quoted prior audit text. These locations document review examples and do not execute Ruby, shell, or any other command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is inside Markdown examples or JSON strings. I found no script file, command runner, or instruction that executes these snippets.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network Library Examples Are Documentation
low_findings.description: The network alerts identify axios imports and api.get examples used to explain import ordering and duplicate error handling. They are sample code only and are not part of executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network-related strings appear in fenced examples and prior audit prose. No active fetch, axios call, HTTP client setup, or exfiltration endpoint exists in the skill files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Non-Crypto Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to YAML frontmatter delimiters, HTTP status code examples, and JSON report text. These are not cryptographic algorithms or hashing operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain metadata, HTTP 200 and 404 constants, or descriptive audit strings. I found no MD5, SHA1, cipher usage, or credential handling.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Code Examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to normal educational snippets such as loop conditions, guard clauses, and function naming examples. They do not query the host, enumerate files, or collect environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is code-quality documentation. There are no commands such as uname, whoami, env listing, directory traversal, or network discovery.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Entropy Alert on Normal Skill Metadata
low_findings.description: The entropy alert targets the start of SKILL.md, which contains ordinary YAML frontmatter and bilingual review guidance. No binary, encrypted payload, packed content, or obfuscated loader was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is plain UTF-8 Markdown with visible metadata and documentation. The higher entropy is plausibly caused by mixed English and Chinese text, not obfuscation.

content.user_title: Review Code for Clean Code Issues
content.value_statement: Code quality problems increase review time and technical debt. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured checklist for finding maintainability issues and suggesting focused refactors.
actual_capabilities: Reviews code against seven Clean Code dimensions: naming, functions, DRY, YAGNI, magic numbers, clarity, and conventions.
Flags vague names, inconsistent terms, long functions, excessive parameters, and mixed naming styles.
Identifies duplicated validation, repeated error handling, dead branches, unused abstractions, and hardcoded values.
Provides severity ratings in high, medium, and low levels for prioritizing refactoring work.
Includes TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, and Go examples for common maintainability patterns.
Outputs findings with principle, location, severity, problem description, and suggested refactoring direction.
limitations: It provides review guidance and does not automatically modify code.
It does not execute tests, compile projects, or prove behavior preservation.
It focuses on maintainability and does not replace security, performance, or architecture reviews.
Its findings depend on the code context and project conventions supplied by the user.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Check changed files for naming, function size, duplication, and hardcoded values before requesting review.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Reviews
use_cases.description: Apply the same Clean Code dimensions across modules and rank issues by severity for team planning.

use_cases.title: Plan Legacy Refactoring
use_cases.description: Find high-value maintainability problems in older code without changing product behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Basic File Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clean-code-reviewer to review this file for naming, function size, duplication, and magic numbers. Return issues by severity.

prompt_templates.title: Focused Refactor Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Review one concern across a folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clean-code-reviewer on src/components. Focus on long functions, excessive parameters, and repeated logic. Include file and line references.

prompt_templates.title: Language Pattern Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clean-code-reviewer for this Python module. Check mutable defaults, bare except blocks, missing type hints, and overly complex comprehensions.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritized Codebase Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use clean-code-reviewer across the repository. Group findings by severity, explain the Clean Code principle, and propose a behavior-preserving refactoring plan.

output_examples.input: Review a utility file with vague variable names and hardcoded retry values.
output: High severity: Replace vague names such as data and result with domain-specific names.
Medium severity: Extract the retry limit into a named constant that explains the business rule.
Suggested next step: Rename variables first, then extract constants in a behavior-preserving change.

output_examples.input: Review a service class with repeated validation and error handling.
output: High severity: Consolidate repeated required-field checks into a shared validation helper.
Medium severity: Extract duplicated API error handling into one wrapper function.
Suggested next step: Add tests around current behavior before refactoring shared paths.

output_examples.input: Review a Go function with deep nesting.
output: Medium severity: Replace nested conditionals with guard clauses for nil and empty input cases.
Low severity: Use clearer helper names for validation branches.
Suggested next step: Refactor structure only, then run the existing test suite.

best_practices: Provide the relevant project conventions before asking for a review.
Ask for file and line references so each issue is easy to verify.
Apply high-severity refactors separately from functional changes.
anti_patterns: Using the skill as a replacement for tests or compiler checks.
Applying every suggestion without checking project context.
Mixing behavior changes with readability refactors in the same change.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews maintainability issues such as naming, function size, duplication, over-engineering, magic numbers, clarity, and project conventions.

faq.question: Does it change my code?
faq.answer: No. It provides findings and refactoring suggestions for the user or agent to apply separately.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The references include TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, and Go examples.

faq.question: Can it review a whole repository?
faq.answer: Yes, but results are best when the user scopes the review by folder, language, or review dimension.

faq.question: Does it find security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on code quality and maintainability, not vulnerability detection.

faq.question: How should I use the severity ratings?
faq.answer: Treat high severity items as priority refactors, medium items as planned improvements, and low items as optional cleanup.
